question.text: Under SEBI regulations, upfront commission from AMCs to mutual fund distributors has been largely abolished. What was the primary regulatory concern that led to this change?

answer.text: Upfront commissions were too expensive for AMCs and reduced their profitability
Upfront commissions created an incentive for distributors to churn portfolios by encouraging unnecessary switches and fresh purchases
Upfront commissions were being paid from investor capital, reducing the NAV at the time of purchase
Upfront commissions violated the AMFI code of conduct regarding maximum compensation limits

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** The primary concern with upfront commissions was churning — distributors had a financial incentive to encourage investors to redeem from one scheme and reinvest in another (or in the same scheme afresh) to earn a new upfront commission each time. This was detrimental to investor interests as frequent switching incurs tax costs and disrupts long-term compounding.
**The Trap:** Option A focuses on AMC costs — the regulatory concern was about investor protection, not AMC profitability. Option C partially describes the mechanism but entry loads (which funded upfront commissions) were abolished separately in 2009. Option D fabricates a specific AMFI rule. The exam wants candidates to identify the investor-protection rationale, specifically the anti-churning objective.
**Cross-Exam Context:** The abolition of upfront commissions was a phased regulatory shift: first, entry loads were banned (2009), then upfront commissions from scheme assets were restricted. The shift to trail-only commissions aligns distributor incentives with long-term investor wealth creation. This is a landmark regulatory change in mutual fund history.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The trail-only model actually benefits good distributors in the long run. Instead of chasing one-time payouts, build a growing AUM book that generates predictable, increasing trail income. Think of it as building an annuity for your business — the bigger and stickier your client base, the more sustainable your income.
